sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** – no documented path for complaints (coach behaviour, billing disputes, facility issues, safety concerns), resolution timelines, or when to involve management vs. resolve floor-side
- **Win-back/churn prevention** – no systematic process for identifying at-risk members, outreach cadence, or retention offers before cancellation
- **Coach performance management & capability gaps** – no SOP for identifying underperforming coaches, coaching them up, or managing them out
- **Financial reconciliation & audit trail** – no monthly close process, refund authorization limits, or how billing exceptions are tracked
- **Health & safety incident log & remediation** – beyond facility maintenance, no process for documenting member/coach injuries, near-misses, or corrective actions
- **New product/class launch** – no playbook for rolling out new offerings (new class type, new time slot, pricing changes)
- **Staffing gaps & cover rota management** – no SOP for managing sick leave, emergency cover, or how decisions are made when short-staffed
- **Seasonal/campaign planning calendar** – no documented rhythm for annual marketing calendar, retention pushes, or promotional windows

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding** – likely missing: what happens if a new member no-shows first class? Follow-up sequence after sign-up? Buddy/community integration steps?
- **Coach training & certification sign-off** – unclear if it covers: what happens when a coach fails assessment, recertification frequency, or how skill gaps (e.g. form correction, cueing) are managed mid-role
- **Class scheduling & substitute protocol** – probably vague on: how far in advance changes are communicated, member notification, backup coach assignment
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – Newsletter and Blog are missing, but

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing is loose — AR/M crashed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June).** You've added 23 net members since relocation but revenue per member fell 29%. Audit membership tiers: introduce a premium "Personal Training Bundle" or "Peak Hours" tier at £25–30/month premium. Migrate existing members at renewal.

• **June revenue £17.7k is down 12% month-on-month despite 20 more active clients.** This screams underpricing or leakage. Implement class pack upsells (10-class bundles at £60–70) and one-off PT sessions (£35–45/session) — non-member revenue line with 80%+ margin.

• **Churn flatlined at 0% in June but was 5.74% in May.** Retention improved; now focus on *filling* the bigger space. You have 147 members in a studio "roughly twice the size" — capacity to reach 200+. Launch a 4-week referral campaign (£20 credit per successful sign-up) targeting existing members' networks. Budget £500; expected payback £3k+.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people expenses spiked to £9,390 in June (53% of total costs, vs 36% May).** This is high. Drill into the £9.4k: is it utilities (heating/lighting for larger space), supplies, rent, or one-off post-relocation costs still settling? If rent increased, model whether you need 170+ members to absorb it. Flag any recurring costs that can be renegotiated (supplier terms, utilities plan).

• **Payroll is stable at £3,686 (June) but has drifted up from £2,514 (Dec).** You've grown 47% in members, so headcount rise is justified — *but* review utilisation. Are all staff scheduled for peak demand hours (6–9pm weekdays, 9am–12pm Sat)? Shift 15% of hours to peak windows; save ~£200–300/month without cutting roles.

## IMPROVE PROFITABILITY & TAX

• **Profit margin collapsed to 5.28% (June) vs 29.29% (May).** The expense spike is the culprit, not revenue softness. Once non-people costs stabilise to ~£7k, you're back to 20%+ margin. **This is your single biggest priority: understand and control the £9.4k

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes urgently:** Her 17 Build: Lower, 9 Build: Upper, 6 Elevate and 3 Strongevity sessions must move to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo already runs Build: Upper successfully; assign her the Lower and Elevate classes. Develop Eilis on Full Body and Strongevity to broaden her range beyond current lighter offerings.

• **Reduce JoJo's PT load to protect class quality:** JoJo carries 44 PTs (two-thirds of all PT) while running 46 classes. Cap her at 25–30 PTs and shift the rest to Eilis, whose current 11 PTs leaves headroom. This prevents fatigue and frees JoJo to mentor Eilis on higher-level programming.

• **Ignore Body Composition Scans in workload planning:** Grace's 9 and JoJo's 20 scans aren't real coaching work. Reassign them to whoever is least busy that week—use them to fill gaps, not to justify heavy schedules.

• **Address Eilis's development bottleneck:** She's currently isolated on lighter classes (Plate, Capacity, Elevate). Pair her with JoJo to shadow and co-coach Build: Upper and Lower before taking them solo—this builds confidence and expands the team's resilience.

• **Rebalance the timetable by day:** JoJo alone shouldn't anchor both Tuesday's technical demand and half the week's PT. Once Eilis gains strength, assign her consistent PT slots and one recurring class per week (e.g. Friday Elevate) to create predictability and prevent burnout.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio gaps between PT and classes:**

1. Launch a weekly paid "Open Studio" slot on Thursday or Monday evenings (your quietest days) — 30-45 mins of unstructured gym access at £5/person. This captures members who want flexibility without committing to a full class, and fills dead studio time with revenue.

2. Run a monthly "Masterclass Workshop" (45 mins, £8-12) on Saturday mornings before or after low-attendance hours — partner with your strongest instructors to deep-dive into form, progression, or nutrition tied to your Build classes. Promotes expertise and drives Saturday footfall.

**For the quietest classes themselves:**

3. Shift **Engine** and **Capacity** to Wednesday or Friday at off-peak hours (early morning 6am or lunch 1pm) to ride the busy-day traffic, then test a "intro week" free pass + buddy incentive — these are likely positioning/cardio work that newer members avoid, so remove entry friction and encourage peer invites.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**Map your current revenue streams first (1-2 hours):**
Audit every service you currently deliver: class types, PT sessions, nutrition coaching, challenges, merchandise, etc. List the actual price each generates. You'll spot gaps and pricing inconsistencies immediately—this is your baseline.

**Build two separate docs, not one:**
- **Coach menu**: Internal pricing, commission structure, what they can/can't sell, upsell sequences (e.g., "after 6 PT sessions, offer nutrition package"). Makes selling easier for your team.
- **Member menu**: Clean pricing, what's *included* in membership tiers, *what's extra*. Use tiered language ("Essential/Pro/Elite") not confusing add-ons. Test this with your current top 5 members first—get feedback before launch.

**Create a visual pricing wall you can actually use:**
Stop at Canva—get a simple A3 laminated poster for your gym floor showing tiers + upgrades. Your coaches should memorize this in 30 minutes. Digital version for Instagram/website takes 1 more hour.

**Price anchor aggressively (this matters):**
Your most popular service/class should NOT be your cheapest—it's your gateway drug. Underprice it now and you'll fight perception forever. If group classes are £8-10, position them as the entry, price 1:1 PT at 3-4x that minimum.

**Lock this in writing with your coaches today:**
Ambiguity kills sales. Coaches need written clarity on commission, what they can offer, what requires your approval. Takes 30 mins, saves months of confusion.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement — Execution Plan for Grace

• **Mine your actual customer conversations first** — Before touching copy, spend 2 hours listening to member onboarding calls or reading WhatsApp/email inquiries. Write down the 5-7 recurring questions prospects ask. These ARE your landing page sections. Don't guess what's "common."

• **Test a radical clarity rewrite on ONE page** — Pick your weakest-converting landing page (probably homepage or main offer). Rewrite the first 100 words to answer "What is The Forge for?" in one sentence + one specific example (e.g., "Women-only strength training for busy professionals" + mention a real member scenario). A/B test it for 2 weeks.

• **Create a "Myths vs Reality" FAQ section** — Women often assume gym culture won't suit them, or they're "too unfit." Build 4-5 FAQ answers directly addressing objections you hear in discovery calls. Put this above the fold on your landing page. This converts better than generic benefits.

• **Audit your funnel's actual flow** — Map where prospects drop off (free trial sign-up? pricing page? class booking?). The landing page refinement only works if the next step is frictionless. Fix bottlenecks in *sequence*, not all at once.

• **Hire a conversion copywriter for 10 hours, not months** — You don't need a full rebrand. Find someone on Fiverr or Upwork with proven gym/fitness experience, brief them on your FAQs + member wins, and have them tighten the messaging. Cheaper than guessing.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System for The Forge

**Make it frictionless—embed it into existing routines:**
- Skip the "form." Add a laminated feedback card slot by the water station + one QR code linking to a 60-second Google Form. Members won't fill out paperwork, but they'll tap their phone while grabbing water post-class. You'll get 3-4x more honest responses.

**Tag feedback to *actionable insights*, not volume:**
- Don't just collect data—route it. Designate one person (you?) to read feedback weekly and categorize into three bins: (1) Class/trainer specific → share with that instructor within 48hrs, (2) Facility issues → maintenance list, (3) Retention risks → flag before members churn. This turns feedback into *action*, not a filing cabinet.

**Use it to compete on community feel:**
- Publicly act on 1-2 pieces of feedback monthly. Post in your member WhatsApp/Instagram: "You asked for later evening classes—we're launching Tuesday 7pm starting [date]." Members see you *listen*. That's worth more than most gyms charge for.

**Time check-ins strategically (not randomly):**
- Monthly post-class chat beats a form. Catch 2-3 members after peak hours (6:30-7:30pm) with genuine questions: "What's working? What's not?" You'll get raw truth + relationship-building in 3 minutes per person.

**Set a metric that matters:**
- Track "member-requested changes implemented" monthly. Aim for 1-2. This is your real KPI—not feedback volume, but speed of response.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Start with YOUR most-complained-about service first**
Don't write all five at once. Pick whichever service generates the most member questions/confusion (likely Personal Training or Fuel Forward based on complexity). Write that SOP in brutal detail, test it with 3 members, refine it. This builds momentum and gives you a template structure for the others.

**2. Structure each SOP identically — use this exact format**
- What they get (what success looks like in 1 sentence)
- Step-by-step member actions (numbered, assumes zero gym knowledge)
- What YOU/staff do at each touchpoint
- Common questions answered upfront
- One person assigned as "SOP owner" who handles updates

This consistency makes them actually usable instead of becoming drawer documents.

**3. Embed these into your member onboarding immediately**
Don't write them then introduce them later. Create a simple checklist: new member gets handed the relevant SOP(s) on Day 1, staff member walks them through it (takes 5 mins max), member signs off they understand. This pressure-tests the SOP in real time and ensures adoption.

**4. Use video + text, not text alone**
For Personal Training and Well75 especially, record a quick 2-3 min phone video of staff walking through one session. Upload to private member portal. Cuts support questions in half and gives members confidence before they arrive.

**5. Version and assign accountability NOW**
Add a date to each SOP and assign one staff member as owner. Schedule a 15-min monthly review. This stops them becoming outdated and gives someone clear responsibility.

Which service is causing

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar for The Forge

**Here's what actually works for a women's gym:**

• **Batch-create content in 90-minute sprints fortnightly** — Film 6-8 videos on one Saturday (member transformations, 60-sec workout demos, behind-the-scenes). Schedule them in Buffer or Later. Takes 2 hours total per month, not daily stress.

• **Use your members as your content factory** — Offer a free month extension for any member who lets you film a 30-sec "why I train here" clip. You'll have authentic testimonials ready to post and rotate. Way more credible than staged content.

• **Template your posts to remove decision paralysis** — Mon = Member spotlight, Wed = 90-sec form tip video, Fri = Weekend class highlight/challenge, Sat = Motivational quote + member photo. Repeat this cycle. You're planning *patterns*, not scrambling for ideas.

• **Make it someone's actual job for 4 hours/week** — Pay a member (or junior staff) £12/hour to caption videos, respond to DMs, and schedule posts. Costs ~£200/month. Without ownership, it dies. Grace won't maintain this alone.

• **Track what drives walk-ins ruthlessly** — Add a simple Google Form QR code in-gym: "How did you hear about us?" After 4 weeks, you'll know if Reels, testimonials, or class clips drive signups. Double down on *that*, ditch the rest.

What's your current posting frequency right now?

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Videography Sessions - Execution Blueprint for Grace

• **Lock it in NOW, not later** — Email Jordan today with 8-week blocks pre-booked for the next 6 months (e.g., Jan 15, Mar 12, May 7). Payment upfront or retainer structure. This removes scheduling friction and signals commitment to him.

• **Give Jordan a creative brief, not a wish list** — Before each session, spend 15 mins defining what *one* key message you're pushing that cycle (new class launch, member transformation story, summer challenge recruitment). One tight theme beats 20 random clips.

• **Batch your content needs** — Each 8-week session should capture 3-4 different video assets (reels, testimonial, class footage, behind-the-scenes). Tell Jordan the breakdown upfront so he optimizes his day and costs stay predictable.

• **Set a distribution calendar the day footage lands** — Decide *now* which video goes to Instagram, TikTok, email, website. Have 2-3 posts pre-scheduled. If you wait to figure this out, videos sit unused for months.

• **Tie it to a revenue goal** — Each cycle should directly support one growth lever (member acquisition, retention, challenge sign-ups, class expansion). After 16 weeks, measure: did this videography drive bookings? If not, pivot the brief or frequency.

What's your biggest bottleneck — getting Jordan locked in, or knowing what to film?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries — Execution Plan

• **Map the current leak first** — Before touching automations, Grace needs to track: How many trial enquiries come in weekly? Where do they drop off (email ignored, no response to follow-up, never book first session)? What's the conversion rate NOW? This tells you what automation actually solves vs. what needs human touch.

• **Build a 3-email sequence, not more** — Day 1: Welcome + instant booking link + "here's what to expect" video (removes friction). Day 3: Social proof (member testimonial video specific to their goal). Day 5: "Last chance" + limited-time offer (creates urgency). Set these in your email tool (Mailchimp, Klaviyo, whatever you use) with conditional logic — if they book, sequence stops.

• **Automate the booking→onboarding handoff** — Once someone books a trial, immediately send calendar confirmation + "arrive 10 mins early, bring trainers" checklist + day-before SMS reminder. This reduces no-shows. Use Zapier or native integrations to trigger these the moment booking is confirmed.

• **Test one micro-change this week** — Don't overhaul everything. Add one element (e.g., that testimonial video on day 3) and measure conversion lift over 2 weeks. Grace will actually know what's working vs. guessing.

• **Owner involvement = first session guarantee** — Automation gets them *to* the gym; Grace needs to own that first session experience (quick chat, setting expectations, showing they care). That's where trial-to-member conversion happens.

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Specific Action Checklist Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with their actual bottlenecks, not job descriptions**
Interview each person for 20 mins: "What takes you 4+ hours weekly that slows growth?" JoJo might say class scheduling, Eilis might say member follow-ups, Sarah might say admin chaos. Build checklists around *their* real friction points, not what sounds good on paper.

**2. Make KPIs backwards from revenue**
Don't assign vanity metrics. Map: JoJo owns "3 new class bookings weekly" (drives £X per month), Eilis owns "10 lapsed-member check-ins" (recovers £Y), Sarah owns "zero missed payment alerts" (protects £Z). Each person sees their direct link to gym revenue.

**3. Use a shared Airtable or Google Sheet they update live, not a PDF**
Weekly = it changes. Embed it where they already work. Set it up so Grace can see in 30 seconds who's on/off track without chasing people. Add a simple RAG status (red/amber/green) per person—forces honesty.

**4. Lock in 15-min Friday check-ins—make them predictable**
Grace reviews actual numbers (not promises), flags blockers same week they happen. "Sarah, payments are at 94% this week—what broke?" Prevents checklist becoming decoration.

**5. Version this within 2 weeks, not "next month"**
Get v1 live in next 7 days, even rough. Spend week 2 adjusting based on what actually works for them in real life.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before you meet:**
- Document your SSO (single, significant objective) in one sentence. Share it via email 48 hours prior so people arrive prepped, not blindsided. Example: "Hit 250 active members by end of Q2 while cutting member churn below 8%."

**Structure the actual session (90 mins max):**
- First 20 mins: You present SSO + why it matters to *them personally* (not just the business). Link it to their job security, bonus potential, or what they care about. Don't read slides.
- Next 35 mins: Walk through the 90-day plan as a visual timeline showing milestones by month. Stop after each month and ask: "What's the blocker you see for *your* role?" Capture these live.
- Final 35 mins: Assign individual "lanes" — be brutally specific. Not "marketing." Say: "Sarah owns member acquisition messaging for Instagram/referral program. Tom owns retention comms via app notifications." Write names next to tasks visible to everyone.

**Close with action:**
- Each person leaves with 3 specific deliverables for month one. Not goals — deliverables with dates. Send a one-pager recap same day so there's zero ambiguity.

**Why this matters for The Forge:** Fitness gyms lose momentum when staff don't know *why* they're doing reps of the same task. Clarity kills the "someone else will handle it" mindset.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge — 30-Day Action Plan

**Get the actual data first (Days 1-3):**
- Have each team member (instructors, reception, management) log their weekly hours, task list, and what takes longest. Don't rely on your gut — spreadsheet it. Include "invisible work" like member complaints, last-minute subs, admin that bleeds into off-hours.
- Compare actual billable/scheduled time vs. total availability. This shows the real squeeze points.

**Identify the over-stretched ones immediately (Day 4):**
- Look for people doing tasks outside their job description (e.g., a receptionist managing social media, an instructor handling scheduling). These are your quick rebalance wins.
- Flag anyone working >40 hours/week consistently — they're your burnout risk and probably your best performers taking on too much.

**Make 2-3 tactical moves in week two:**
- **Delegate down:** Can instructors handle their own class admin instead of reception doing it? Can a motivated newer team member take one recurring admin task off your manager?
- **Batch the work:** Consolidate member admin tasks (queries, payments, sign-ups) into two fixed windows daily so it's not constant interruption.
- **Automate or kill it:** What tasks genuinely don't move the needle? (e.g., manual attendance tracking if your booking system captures it). Stop doing them.

**By day 30, lock in the new structure:**
- Have each person confirm their revised role/hours in writing. Prevents backslide and shows respect for the change.
- Schedule a quick monthly check-in to catch drift early.

# Cut 10% Costs at The Forge — Concrete Plays

• **Audit your biggest three expense buckets first** — Rent/utilities, staff payroll, and equipment maintenance likely account for 60-70% of spend. You don't need to nickel-and-dime across everything; identify which ONE of these three can realistically drop 3-4% without hurting member experience. A 2% utility reduction + 4% payroll optimization + 3% supplier renegotiation = your 10%.

• **Renegotiate supplier contracts THIS WEEK** — Call your equipment maintenance, cleaning, and inventory suppliers. You have leverage now (existing relationship). Lead with: "I'm doing a cost audit and need your best rate or I'm testing alternatives." Most will drop 5-8% without losing quality. Takes 2-3 hours of calls, saves real money immediately.

• **Cut one shift or cross-train staff strategically** — If you have a slow-traffic hour (likely early morning or late evening), can you consolidate that shift into another? Or train one existing PT to cover reception 1-2 hours daily? Payroll is flexible; don't fire, just restructure. This alone could hit 3-5% savings.

• **Review your marketing spend ruthlessly** — Are you getting ROI on paid ads, or throwing money at vanity channels? Pause the lowest-performing platform for 30 days and redirect that budget to what's actually converting (likely word-of-mouth or direct outreach to local corporate wellness). Quick 10-15% marketing trim with zero member impact.

• **Audit contracted services** — Are you paying for something you don't use regularly? Accounting,

# Update Monthly Finance KPIs — Execution Blueprint for Grace

• **Audit your current metrics TODAY** — Open your last 3 months of data. What are you *actually* tracking vs. what you *should* be? Most gym owners track revenue only. You need: member LTV, churn rate, class utilization %, cost per acquisition, and cash position. Pick 5-7 max or you'll ignore them.

• **Set the 15-minute weekly ritual, not monthly** — Monthly reviews are too late to course-correct. Every Monday morning, Grace spends 15 mins pulling: weekly revenue, new sign-ups, cancellations, and cash balance. Feed this into a simple Google Sheet or Stripe dashboard. Monthly updates become *validations* of trends you already know.

• **Tie KPIs directly to one growth lever each** — "Revenue up 12% YoY" is useless. Instead: *"Member retention at 87% (target: 90%) → action: audit class schedule this week"* and *"CAC at £45 (target: £35) → action: review which marketing channel recruited those members."* Every number must trigger a decision.

• **Benchmark against YOUR baseline, not competitors** — Don't obsess over industry standards yet. What were your numbers 6 months ago? That's your real competition. Month-on-month growth of 3-5% in members is solid for a niche gym; chase that consistency first.

• **Assign the data entry to someone else NOW** — Grace, you shouldn't be entering numbers. Train a team member (even 2 hours/month) to pull and format data. You review and interpret only. Frees

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Build a 3-statement model in Excel TODAY** — P&L, cash flow, balance sheet. Start with last 12 months actuals, then project 24 months forward. Use membership revenue as your anchor (fixed/variable split), cost of goods as % of revenue, staffing as your biggest variable. This takes 2-3 hours, not days.

• **Run three scenarios: conservative (10% growth), base case (25% growth), aggressive (40% growth)** — Tie each to a specific action (e.g., "10% = current marketing spend, 25% = hire 1 PT, 40% = second location feasibility"). This forces you to link numbers to decisions you'll actually make.

• **Identify your cash crunch months NOW** — Gyms typically dip Jan-Feb and summer. Project when you'd hit zero cash and by how much. This tells you exactly how much working capital or credit line you need before growth stalls.

• **Track 5 KPIs weekly in a simple dashboard** — Revenue per member, churn rate, cost per acquisition, payroll %, rent as % of revenue. When forecasts diverge from actual, you'll spot it within days, not months.

• **Share the conservative scenario with your bank/accountant THIS WEEK** — You need to know your borrowing capacity for growth investment *before* you need it. Credibility comes from having numbers ready.

What's your current monthly membership count and average revenue per member?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge – Execution Plan

**1. Start with your three revenue buckets – don't overthink it**
Pull last month's income statements separated by: group classes (revenue ÷ # sessions), personal training (# sessions × average rate), add-ons (merchandise, supplements, plans). You likely already have this in your booking system or invoices – just categorize it. Takes 30 minutes, not a spreadsheet masterpiece.

**2. Calculate true marginal cost per service type**
- **Classes**: instructor cost + facility allocation (rent/utilities ÷ weekly class slots). Rough it – if rent is £3k/month and you run 40 class slots/week, that's ~£17 facility cost per slot.
- **PT**: instructor take-home % + any PT-specific insurance. Simplest calculation here.
- **Add-ons**: cost of goods sold (what you actually paid for that water bottle or protein bar) – already should be on invoices.

**3. Map contribution margin, not just gross profit**
For each service: Revenue minus variable costs = what's left to cover overhead. Classes might have lower unit margin but fill capacity. PT might have higher margin but requires scheduling friction. Add-ons might be 60%+ margin if they're impulse buys. This tells you *which generates actual cash*.

**4. Identify your one quick win in the next 7 days**
Once mapped, you'll spot it: maybe add-ons are criminally unprofitable (reorder from cheaper supplier) or classes are surprisingly high-margin (run more peak-time slots). Act on that first to prove the exercise works.

**

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan: Automation Audit for The Forge

• **Map the current flow visually TODAY** — Export your automation triggers/actions into a simple spreadsheet (what triggers what, in what order). You'll spot duplicates immediately. Look especially for multiple "new enquiry" triggers firing the same welcome email or SMS — common culprit in growing gyms.

• **Test the full journey as a fake prospect** — Have someone sign up as a new member through your website right now. Does she get the welcome sequence? Does she receive trial booking link? Does she actually get invited to a specific trial slot? If ANY step breaks or goes silent, that's your leak. Document exactly where it fails.

• **Kill the redundant automations ruthlessly** — If you have 3 welcome sequences or 2 different trial reminder flows, delete the weaker one completely. Duplication kills conversion (confused messaging + inbox fatigue). Keep ONE clean pathway: enquiry → welcome → trial offer → confirmation → post-trial follow-up.

• **Audit the trial-to-paid conversion step specifically** — Most gyms lose money here. After trial, is there an automation reminding her about membership pricing? Is it timed right (not same day, but within 48 hours)? If not, add it now — this single step often doubles trial-to-paid rates.

• **Set a quarterly review trigger** — Block 90 minutes next quarter to repeat this. Automations drift. You'll add new ones and forget old ones exist.

**Done by end of week?** You'll know exactly where enquiries drop off and have a cleaner, faster conversion machine.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Play

**Pick these 3 tasks (highest ROI):**
- **New member onboarding emails** (Welcome → class booking → first check-in) — automate the sequence based on signup date/membership tier. Use Zapier + ChatGPT to personalize tone for your audience. *Why:* Runs weekly, takes 30+ mins, directly impacts retention.
- **Instagram caption + hashtag generation** — feed 3 posts/week into Claude with your brand voice + current promotions. Spit out 5 caption options + Belfast fitness hashtags in 2 mins. *Why:* You're probably redrafting these; AI nails consistency.
- **Inquiry response template logic** — route common questions (class times, pricing, trial offer) to AI-first response via email or WhatsApp. Human review takes 10 secs. *Why:* Phone/email inquiry volume kills your admin time.

**Execute this week:**
1. **Use Make.com** (formerly Integromat) — cheaper than Zapier for beginners, visual workflows. Connect your email provider → ChatGPT API. Test onboarding sequence with one fake signup first.
2. **Don't build perfect.** Start messy. Onboarding v1 can be 70% automated; you edit the last 30% for personality.
3. **Measure the time freed:** Track hours saved this month. If you reclaim 5 hours/week, that's capacity for sales calls or content planning—the real growth moves.

Which of these three feels most painful right now?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Implementation for The Forge Website

**Right now, today:**

• **Audit what you're actually vulnerable to** — Run a free SSL checker (sslshopper.com), check if you're collecting payment/member data, and identify what would actually hurt your business if breached. Don't secure everything — secure what matters (member sign-ups, payment processing, class booking data). This takes 30 mins and tells you if you need basic fixes or heavy lifting.

• **Get an SSL certificate live immediately** (if you don't have one) — This is non-negotiable for trust + Google ranking. If you're on WordPress/Wix/Squarespace, it's 1-click free. If you're on custom code, budget £50-200/year. This protects member login credentials in transit.

• **Lock down admin access NOW** — Change your website backend password to 16+ characters, enable two-factor authentication on your hosting/CMS, and remove any default usernames. Most breaches happen here, not fancy attacks. Takes 15 mins.

• **Set a monthly 30-min maintenance rhythm** — Updates to plugins/themes, checking for suspicious login attempts, backing up your member database. One breach costs you £5-10K in reputation + legal. Spending 2 hours/month prevents it.

• **Document what you've done** — Screenshot your SSL cert, 2FA setup, and backup location. You'll need this for insurance and member trust if anything happens.

Which platform is The Forge website built on? That changes whether this is DIY-able or worth hiring someone (£300-800 one-time).

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost per lead has quadrupled since April (£6 → £27) while lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 → 4). July's spend cut to a quarter of previous months signals either a budget pause or account issue, not optimization. The 75% close rate in July is your best conversion rate ever—the problem isn't sales quality, it's lead scarcity. You're generating profit on skeletal volume; scale back up and you'd be printing money.

The April-to-July decay suggests ad fatigue, audience saturation in your tight Belfast women's fitness market, or creative that stopped resonating. Your core offer clearly works (60+ sales across four months), but your ads stopped reaching people.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately restore budget to £400–500/month** and split it across two ad sets: one retesting your April-winning creative (the one that hit £6/lead), one with fresh creative angles—test "before/after transformations at The Forge," testimonial videos from recent converters, and "no-gym-experience" messaging if that wasn't in rotation. Run both for two weeks; kill underperformers.

2. **Widen targeting beyond core audience.** April's broad reach (69 leads on £435) suggests you weren't over-segmented. Test lookalike audiences built from your 20 April sales converters, expand age range to 25–55 if currently narrower, and layer in "fitness enthusiasts" + "health & wellness interested" rather than just "gym" keywords. Belfast's small; you may have exhausted obvious prospects.

3. **Test a different offer hook.** "6-week trial" works, but July's best close rate hints your message quality improved—lean into what changed in July creative. If you pivoted to "judgment-free," "women-only community," or specific pain-point language (nervous about gyms, post-baby fitness, etc.), double down. Run that against your old April creative in a proper A/B.

4. **Cut creative every 2–3 weeks.** Once cost per lead hits £20+, your audience sees the ad fatigue. Schedule monthly creative refreshes—don't wait for collapse. July's 4 leads
